coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/ Cu2O S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junli
Li, Shuying
Ma, Pengyi
Guo, Zhinan
Ma, Qi
Zhao, Qiang
Guo, Yong
Zhao, Jianguo
Guan, Guoqing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with yellow fluorescence were firstly prepared by hydrothermal method using corn stover as raw material. Then, the CQDs/Cu2O S-scheme heterojunction was obtained by combining the prepared CQDs with dodecahedral Cu2O. The catalytic performance of the prepared catalyst under visible light was studied by using tetracycline (TC) as the degradation target. The results showed that the conductivity and the active sites in Cu2O were enhanced by loading CQDs, and the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction showed the highest photocatalytic performance and the most excellent stability with 92.49% of TC degradation within 100 min. It is found that the recombination rate of electron and hole was reduced by the CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction with improved separation efficiency of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs and enhanced photo-degradation efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible-light photocatalysts
CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction
Photocatalysis degradation
Tetracycline antibiotics

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image, (b) XRD pattern, (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) Photoluminescence spectrum of CQDs; and element mappings of (e) C and (f) O of CQDs; and (g) EDS of CQDs.

SEM images of (a) Cu2O particle and (b) 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O; and (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite; and element mappings of (e) C, (f) O and (g) Cu of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite; and (h) EDS of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra of Cu2O and various CQDs/Cu2O composites with different amounts of CQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of C 1 s(b), O 1 s (c) and Cu 2p (d) of Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite.
(a) UV-Vis DRS spectra of CQDs, Cu2O and various CQDs/Cu2O composites with different amounts of CQDs; and (b) band gaps of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite.
Mott-Schotty plots of (a) CQDs and (b) Cu2O.
(a) UPS spectra of CQDs and Cu2O and (b) energy band arrangements of CQDs and Cu2O before contact, after contact in darkness, and contact under illumination.
Photoluminescence spectra of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O composite.
(a) Photocurrent responses and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation curves of TC; (b) Repeated photocatalytic degradation of TC over 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite and (c) The fitting curve of ln(C/C0) versus t.

(a) SEM image of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite photocatalyst after the photo-degradation of TC for 5-cycle testing and (b) XRD patterns of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite catalyst before and after the photo-degradation of TC for five-cycle testing.
(a) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite with the irradiation for 5 min in methanol dispersion (for DMPO-·O2 -) and (b) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-·OH).
Active species trapping experiments with 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite under visible light irradiation (IPA: Isopropyl alcohol).
Photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/Cu2O composite under visible light.
The schematic diagram for the preparations of (a) CQDs, (b) Cu2O and (c) CQDs/ Cu2O composite.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/ Cu2O S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Engineering Research Center of Coal-based Ecological Carbon Sequestration Technology of the Ministry of Education, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Engineering Research Center of Coal-based Ecological Carbon Sequestration Technology of the Ministry of Education, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Conversion Engineering Laboratory, Institute of Regional Innovation (IRI), Hirosaki University, 3-Bunkyocho, Hirosaki 036-8561, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Energy Conversion Engineering Laboratory, Institute of Regional Innovation (IRI), Hirosaki University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Conversion Engineering Laboratory, Institute of Regional Innovation (IRI), Hirosaki University, 3-Bunkyocho, Hirosaki 036-8561, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with yellow fluorescence were firstly prepared by hydrothermal method using corn stover as raw material. Then, the CQDs/Cu2O S-scheme heterojunction was obtained by combining the prepared CQDs with dodecahedral Cu2O. The catalytic performance of the prepared catalyst under visible light was studied by using tetracycline (TC) as the degradation target. The results showed that the conductivity and the active sites in Cu2O were enhanced by loading CQDs, and the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction showed the highest photocatalytic performance and the most excellent stability with 92.49% of TC degradation within 100 min. It is found that the recombination rate of electron and hole was reduced by the CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction with improved separation efficiency of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs and enhanced photo-degradation efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have high efficiency in the treatment of infectious diseases [1]. However, with the misuse of antibiotics in modern society, environmental pollution caused by it is becoming more and more serious [2]. The routes of transmission of antibiotics in the environment include medical wastewater, farm wastewater, agricultural discharges and municipal wastewater, in which antibiotics are always prevalent in at concentrations above environmental standards. Especially, the widespread use of antibiotics leads to increased bacterial resistance, excessive antibiotic residues in animal products and negative impacts on ecosystems [3]. Antibiotic contaminants in wastewater are always the concerns of researchers [4]. Photocatalytic process is one of the current technologies with high efficiency and saving energy for the removal of pollutant from water [5].

Cu2O, as a semiconductor with narrow band gap, can be used as a visible light catalyst, which can catalyze the degradation of organic pollutants using solar energy [6]. However, as a single semiconductor material, its photo-generated electron-hole pairs are usually prone to recombine rapidly, reducing the quantum efficiency of the catalyst, which limits its application in photocatalysis field [7]. To solve this issue, it is found that constructing an effective heterojunction structure is an effective method to improve the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor materials [8]. Semiconductors have unique energy bands and there are also differences in the energy bands even in the similar semiconductors [9]. Heterojunctions have been successfully synthesized by combining semiconductors with different energy bands to form new energy bands [10,11], which can improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron pair and reduce the recombination rate, leading to excellent performance [12].

CQDs are quasi-spherical zero-dimensional carbon materials [13], which have the advantages of strong photo-luminescence, high electrical conductivity, broad excitation spectrum and high chemical stability [14]. The raw materials for the preparation of CQDs come from a wide range of sources, such as seeds [15], Andrographis paniculatas [16] and watermelon rinds [17]. CQDs have been applied in cellular imaging [18], fluorescence sensing [19], drug delivery [20], electrochemistry [21], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [22], energy storage [10] and photocatalysis [23]. CQDs-based heterojunctions have received much attention as a highly attractive alternative of conventional photocatalytic materials [13]. The polymerization of CQDs during the photocatalytic process leads to the loss of active sites on the surface [18]. Therefore, the photocatalytic property of CQDs is limited [23]. However, the photocatalytic properties of CQDs can be improved by incorporating an oxide layer, combining with other materials, coupling with metallic/non-metallic catalysts, morphology tuning, heterojunction formation or cocatalysts incorporation [24].

In this work, the CQDs were innovatively prepared by using corn stover as a raw material firstly, then, various CQDs/Cu2O composites were obtained and used for degradation of tetracycline (TC). The degradation performance and photocatalytic mechanism of TC under visible light were investigated using the CQDs/Cu2O composite as photocatalyst.

CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using Corn Stover (harvest from Datong, China) as biomass carbon resource. The preparation process of CQDs was shown in Scheme 1 a. Generally, Corn stover powder (50 g), NaOH solution (450 mL, 5 mol/L) and a rotor were added into a flask. The solution was refluxed at 120°C for 2 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min at 5 ℃ to remove those lager particles. The orange-colored solution showed blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp. The pH was adjusted to 5 using 5 mol/L HCl, then add 600 mL of ethanol and leave it to stay overnight. The solution was filtered under reduced pressure to remove impurities and concentrated to 180 mL using a rotary evaporator, then filtered, sealed and frozen in a refrigerator. The solution was dried to a solid using a freeze dryer, and the solid was removed and powdered to obtain 0.92 g of CQDs.

The preparation process of Cu2O was illustrated in Scheme 1 b. Distilled water (83.4 mL), CuCl2 solution (5 mL, 0.5 mol/L) and ethanol (20 mL) were added to a 500 mL beaker with a rotor. The solution was stirred at 40℃ for 30 min. 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 9 mL (1.0 mol/L) NaOH were added respectively. After that, 9.8 mL (0.5 mol/L) of hydroxylamine hydrochloride was quickly added to the beaker, stirring for 10 min. The solution was left at 40℃ for 30 min and filtered. Finally, the complex was washed with ethanol and dried at 40 °C for 12 h. As such, the dodecahedral Cu2O was obtained.

The preparation of CQDs/Cu2O composite was shown in Scheme 1 c. 20 mL of ethanol, 0.100 g of Cu2O and 0.005 g of CQDs were added into a clean brown bottle. The mixture was ultrasound-treated for 60 min. Herein, the temperature should not exceed 30 ℃. After ultrasound-treating, the 5 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite was obtained after drying at 40 ℃. Similarly, other CQDs/Cu2O composites with different amounts of CQDs were obtained and marked as x% CQDs/Cu2O.

To study the catalyst microstructure and morphology, scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were analyzed on TESCAN MAIA 3 LMH apparatus and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were analyzed on JEOL, JEM-2999FMII apparatus. The crystal structures of the catalysts were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on a testing instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi system). FT-IR spectrum was measured by using a CDS-2000 Bio-Rad FTS-165 detector. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) analysis was done with Jena SPECORD® 210 PLUS. The photocurrent responses were studied in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots were obtained in a mixed solution (0.1 M KCl and 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-). Mott-Schotty plots were obtained in a solution containing 0.5 mM Na2SO4. The photoluminescence (PL) was studied by Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, with was excited at 300 nm. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was conducted using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe instrument (ULVAC-PHI, Japan) equipped with a photon beam generated at an energy of 21.2 eV (He I) by a Physical Electronics source. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was carried out with A300 BRUKE Plused Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectrometer.

The activity evaluation was done under the irradiation of a Xe lamp. Under stirring, 100 mg catalyst was introduced into 90 mL TC (10 mg/L). The adsorption equilibrium on tetracycline was achieved by stirring the solution in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, the TC solution was irradiated with visible light under stirring. Every 10 min, 4 mL of solution was extracted, then, the solids were removed by centrifugation and the liquid was tested with a 722E spectrometer at 357 nm.

Fig. 1a shows the HRTEM image of CQDs, which shows that CQDs have a spherical structure with a diameter of 5 nm. Fig. 1b shows the XRD patterns of CQDs, in which a diffraction peak near 2θ=23.9◦ may be related to the (002) crystalline plane of graphite (JCPDS PDF#74–2328), illustrating that CQDs has high catalytic activity potential [25]. Fig. 1c shows the FTIR spectrum of CQDs, where the characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs were observed. The peak at 3428 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O-H bond whereas the peaks at 2926 and 1040 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C-H and C-O bonds, respectively. Generally, in a photocatalytic reaction, the activity is closely correlated with the -OH amount on the surface of catalyst since -OH can trap holes and convert them into reactive ·OH. Herein, ·OH is an important free radical for the decomposition of organic molecules [26]. Fig. 1d shows the photoluminescence spectrum of CQDs. When the fluorescence emission range was 315–850 nm and the excitation wavelength was 300 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of CQDs was 405 nm, which is agreement with the results obtained by Zhou et al. [24]. The excellent photoluminescence performance of the CQDs could promote photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 1e-g show the elemental mapping diagram and EDS diagram of CQDs. Obviously, CQDs contains C and O elements.

Fig. 2a shows SEM image of Cu2O, in which the particle sizes of Cu2O are between 300 ∼ 400 nm with a dodecahedral structure [27]. Figs. 2b and 2c show SEM and TEM images of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O. One can see that the surface of Cu2O is covered by CQDs and the shape of Cu2O is not changed after loading CQDs. Fig. 2d shows HRTEM image of the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O. Obviously, Cu2O and CQDs are combined well, where the spacing of 0.25 nm corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of Cu2O [28], and the spacing of 0.33 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [29]. These results indicate that the lattice structure can remain unchanged after the combination of Cu2O and CQDs. Fig. 2e-h show the elemental mapping diagram and EDS diagram of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite, in which Cu, C and O elements are distributed well.

Fig. 3a shows XRD patterns of Cu2O and the CQDs/Cu2O composite, which can confirm crystal phases of the synthesized composites. The peaks at 2θ of 29.7°, 36.5°, 42.5°, 61.6° and 73.7° correspond to the (110), (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of Cu2O. The XRD pattern of Cu2O was confirmed by the standard card (JCPDS PDF#77–0199) [30].

Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O composites. In the spectrum of Cu2O, the peak at 630 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Cu(I)-O bond [31]. For the CQDs/Cu2O composite, the peaks at 3428, 2926, and 1040 cm−1 can be classified as the characteristic peaks of CQDs (Fig. 1c). The peaks of CQDs and Cu2O are observed in the spectra of CQDs/Cu2O, indicating the presence of CQDs and Cu2O in the composite.

Fig. 4a shows the total XPS spectrum of Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite, in which the elements C, O and Cu are presented in the total XPS spectra. Fig. 4b shows the C 1 s spectra of Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite. Here, the C 1 s peak in pure Cu2O is ascribed to the compensated carbon. The peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.3 eV indicate the presence of C-H, C-OH, and O-CO bonds in the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O, respectively [32]. Fig. 4c shows the O 1 s specta of Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite. The characteristic peak at 530.5 eV corresponds to the binding energy of Cu2O whereas the peak at 532.1 eV is caused by hydroxyl oxygen on the surface of Cu2O [33]. Fig. 4d shows the Cu 2p spectra of Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite, in which the two peaks at 932.3 and 952.2 eV correspond to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [33]. The above results also indicate that the CQDs and Cu2O have been successfully combined in the CQDs/Cu2O composite. Compared with pure Cu2O, the O 1 s and Cu 2p peaks of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O shift toward the lower binding energy. In general, the decrease of binding energy indicates the increasement of electron density, attributing to the acquisition of electrons by the atoms. Similarly, the increasement of binding energy could be attributed to the loss of electrons from the atoms [34]. Therefore, the lower binding energies of O 1 s and Cu 2p in Cu2O indicate the electron migration from CQDs to Cu2O in the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O with a built-in electric field formed between CQDs and Cu2O, resulting in positively charged CQDs and negatively charged Cu2O, which is consistent with the S-scheme mechanism.

Fig. 5a shows the UV-Vis absorption spectroscopies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O composite. Here, Cu2O has an absorption edge around 630 nm, and when CQDs was combined with Cu2O, the absorption edge tends to move towards the UV region, which could enhance the absorption intensity. Fig. 5b shows the band gaps of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite. By calculations, the band gaps of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite are 2.08, 2.13 and 2.04 eV, respectively. Obviously, the combination of CQDs and Cu2O has reduced the band gap and improved the absorption of visible light. This facilitated electronic transition could lead to the enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance.

Fig. 6 shows the Mott-Schottky plots of CQDs and Cu2O. As shown in Fig. 6, the flat-band potentials (Eg) of CQDs and Cu2O are −0.78 and −0.5 eV, respectively [33,43]. The slopes of curves are positive, which indicates that both CQDs and Cu2O are n-type semiconductors[35–37]. Usually, the conduction band (CB) is negative by 0.1–0.2 eV compared to Eg for the n-type semiconductor [38]. Herein, when 0.2 eV is considered, the CBs of CQDs and Cu2O are −0.98 and −0.7 eV, respectively. According to the formula: EVB=ECB+Eg (Fig. 5b), it could be concluded that the valence band potentials (VBs) of CQDs and Cu2O are 1.1 and 1.43 eV, respectively [39]. Such VB and CB of Cu2O and CQDs also suggest that S-scheme heterojunction could have been constructed between Cu2O and CQDs.

Fig. 7a shows the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopies (UPSs) of CQDs and Cu2O. Herein, based on the work function of Φ=hv+Ecutoff-EF [40], the Φs of CQDs and Cu2O are 5.7 and 6.7 eV, respectively. The EF values of CQDs and Cu2O are 18.9 and 17.0 eV, respectively. That is, the Cu2O has a higher Φ with a lower EF whereas CQDs have a smaller Φ with a higher EF. As shown in Fig. 7b, after combining Cu2O with CQDs, in darkness, the e- in CQDs will transfer to Cu2O until achieving a balanced EF [41]. The internal electric field (IEF) between the negatively charged Cu2O and the positively charged CQDs should be built up due to the charge redistribution and high chemical interaction at interfaces. Meanwhile, the band edge of CQDs bends could be raised by the loss of e- and that of Cu2O bends decreased by the aggregation of e-. As such, under visible light, Cu2O and CQDs will be excited simultaneously, and the energy band bending and IEF can promote the combination of the e- generated in CB of Cu2O with h+ generated in VB of CQDs. Consequently, it becomes the typical S-scheme heterojunction between Cu2O and CQDs.

Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence spectra of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O composite. Generally, the lower the intensity of emission peak is, the lower the recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pair is. The peak intensity of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O is lower than that of either Cu2O or CQDs, indicating that the combination of Cu2O and CQDs has optimized the photo-generated electron-hole separation performance of the photocatalyst. The photo-generated electron-hole pair recombination rate gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs loading and the degradation properties have been gradually improved. The intensity of the emission peaks is decreased significantly from 1 wt% CQDs/Cu2O to 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O. The specific surface area is decreased when the loading amount is greater than 3 wt%. Also, the amount of active sites is reduced and the photo-generated electron-hole pair recombination rate is increased. The intensity of the emission peaks increases significantly from 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O to 15 wt% CQDs/Cu2O. These results are in accordance with the photocatalytic degradation curves analysis results (Fig. 10a).

Fig. 9a shows the photocurrent responses of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite, in which the photocurrent value of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O is significantly higher than those of Cu2O and CQDs, indicating that the e--h+ pairs of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite can be separated effectively [42]. Fig. 9b shows the EIS Nyquist plots of CQDs, Cu2O and 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O, in which the circle radius of the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite is smaller than those of the Cu2O and CQDs, indicating that 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O has a small electrical resistance and a large interfacial charge electron transfer rate. Therefore, the CQDs/Cu2O composite should have a stronger ability to transfer photo-generated electrons[43].

The photocatalytic degradation curves of TC are shown in Fig. 10a. The degradation of TC by 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite is 92.49% within 100 min. Obviously, the catalytic activity of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O is higher than those of pure Cu2O and CQDs. When the loading amount of CQDs is increased, the performance of the CQDs/Cu2O composite for TC degradation decreases. Fig. 10b shows the stability of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite. It can be seen that the degradation rate of TC by 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite can maintain at 88.8% through 5 cycles, which indicates that the incorporation of CQDs can greatly improve the stability of the catalyst. Moreover, the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite has good reusability, indicating its potential application. Fig. 10c shows the fitted curve of ln(C/C0) versus t for the degradation of TC by the prepared photocatalyst. In(C/C0)=-kt, where the concentrations of TC at 0 and t min are C0 and C, respectively. The slope k is the apparent reaction rate constant. ln(C0/C) and t are linearly related, which is the characteristic of the first-order kinetic equation. The results show that the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction has the largest value of k, which indicates that it has the best degradation property [44].

Fig. 11a shows the SEM image of the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite catalyst for the photo-degradation of TC after 5-cycle tests. One can see that the CQDs are still attached to the dodecahedral Cu2O. Fig. 11b shows the XRD pattern of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite after the degradation of TC for 5 cycles. No CuO and Cu species have been detected in the spent catalyst, which means that the crystal structure of Cu2O remains unchanged.

Fig. 12 shows the EPR spectrum of 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite, which is used to identify the free radicals (·OH and ·O2 -) generated by the system, investigating the synergistic mechanism of CQDs and Cu2O in promoting the degradation of TC. The DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH signals are observed in the EPR spectra, which indicates that large amounts of ·OH and ·O2 - can be produced on the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite under visible light. Notably, the EPR intensity of DMPO-·O2 - is stronger than that of DMPO-·OH, indicating that more ·O2 - species are produced. Because of the synergistic effect of CQDs and Cu2O, the best visible light utilization and high electron separation characteristics are realized.

Fig. 13 shows the results of quenching experiment of active species for 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O composite. The degradation rate of TC is changed after adding the IPA (·OH scavenger), indicating that ·OH is an important active specie in the degradation process. In addition, the catalytic activity is reduced significantly under N2 atmosphere, indicating that ·O2 - is a major species involved in the degradation process.

Fig. 14 shows the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/Cu2O S-scheme heterostructure under visible light. From Figs. 4–13, the electrons of CQDs/Cu2O composite on valence band (VB) are more easily excited to the conduction band (CB) under visible light. The incorporation of CQDs enhances the absorption of visible light, which leads to more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs. While, more photo-generated electrons are generated in Cu2O and react with O2 to form more ·O2 - whereas more holes are generated and react with H2O and -OH to form more ·OH. At the same time, the separated electrons from the CQDs can also react with O2 to form ·O2 -. As such, the TC is decomposed by the resulting ·O2 - and ·OH into CO2, H2O and other small molecules. Consequently, the CQDs/Cu2O heterostructure promotes the separation of photo-generated holes and electrons by inhibiting the recombination of electron transfer carriers inside Cu2O so that the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts is improved greatly.

In this study, CQDs was firstly prepared by a hydrothermal method using corn stover as the raw material. Then, CQDs/Cu2O S-scheme heterojunction was designed and synthesized and its photocatalytic performance for TC degradation was investigated. The results showed that 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction exhibited the best catalytic degradation performance with a TC degradation rate of 92.49% in 100 min under visible light. Moreover, the 3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O heterojunction had better catalytic degradation performance than either CQDs or Cu2O. It is found that CQDs/Cu2O composite showed low recombination rate of electron and hole, resulting better electron-hole separation. In addition, the degradation performance had no significant decrease after five cycles of repeated uses. This study contributes to the application of Cu2O-based photocatalysts in wastewater treatment.

Guoqing Guan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Junli Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jianguo Zhao: Validation, Investigation. Qiang Zhao: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Yong Guo: Validation, Investigation. Shuying Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Pengyi Ma: Validation, Investigation. Zhinan Guo: Validation, Investigation. Qi Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21908135), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (201901D111308, 201901D211435)，Ph.D. Research Startup Foundation of Shanxi Datong University (2018-B-01，2020-B-02).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Omadacycline: a new tetracycline antibiotic
Ann. Pharmacother.
